volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Stichus (Section 1.1.1 - Section 1.2.108)

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Crédo egó miserám
sections.translation: Sister , I think that Penelope was wretched from her very soul , who was so long deprived of her husband ; for from our own fortunes , whose husbands are absent from us , we judge of her feelings ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: quorúmque nos negótiis apséntum , ita ut aéquom est ,
sections.translation: for whose affairs , still , in their absence , both night and day , sister , as is becoming , we are ever anxious .

sections.label: Section 1.1.7
sections.original: Nostrum ófficium
sections.translation: ’ Tis right that we should do our duty ; and we do not that any further than affection bids us .

sections.label: Section 1.1.9
sections.original: sed híc , soror , assíde dum : multá volo tecum
sections.translation: But , sister , step this way a moment ; I want

sections.label: Section 1.1.10
sections.original: loquí de re viri .
sections.translation: to speak about the affairs of my husband .

sections.label: Section 1.1.10b
sections.original: Salvéne , amabo ?
sections.translation: Ain ’ t they prospering , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.11
sections.original: Speró quidem et volo ; sed hóc , soror , crúcior ,
sections.translation: I hope and wish so , indeed . But , sister , at this am I vexed , that your and my father , one who is esteemed as especially honorable among all his fellow-citizens , should be now acting the part of a dishonorable man ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.original: virís qui tantas apséntibus nóstris
sections.translation: who is undeservedly doing so great an injustice to our absent husbands , and is wishing to separate us from them . These things , sister , render me tired of existence ; these things are a care and a vexation to me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.original: Ne lácruma , soror , neu túo id animo
sections.translation: Weep not , sister , nor do that to your feelings which your father is threatening to do . ’ Tis to be hoped [*] that he will act more righteously . I know him well ; he says these things in jest ; and he would not earn for himself the mountains of the Persians ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: montís , qui esse aurei pérhibentur ,
sections.translation: which are said to be of gold [*] , to do that of which you are in dread . Still , if he does do it , it befits you by no means to be angry ; nor will it happen without some reason .

sections.label: Section 1.1.29
sections.original: nam víri nostri domo ut ábierunt ,
sections.translation: For this is the third year since our husbands have been away from home .

sections.label: Section 1.1.31
sections.original: Quom ipsi ínterea vivánt , valeant ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis as you say ; while , in the meantime , they may be living , and may be well [*] , they do not make us acquainted where they are , what they are doing , whether they are doing well , neither do they return .

sections.label: Section 1.1.34_35
sections.original: An id dóles , soror , quia illi suom ófficium
sections.translation: And do you , sister , regret this , that they do not observe their duty , whereas you do yours ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.36b
sections.original: Ita pol .
sections.translation: Troth , I do .

sections.label: Section 1.1.37
sections.original: Tace sís , cave sis audíam ego istuc
sections.translation: Hold your peace , if you please ; take care , please , that I hear not that same thing from you in future .

sections.label: Section 1.1.38b
sections.original: Nam quíd iam ?
sections.translation: And why , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.39
sections.original: Quia pól meo animo omnis sápientis
sections.translation: Because , i ’ faith , in my opinion , ’ tis proper for all prudent people

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: suom offícium aequom est colere ét facere .
sections.translation: to observe and to do their duty . For that reason , sister , although you are the older , I advise you to remember your duty ; and if they are unjust and act otherwise to us than is right , then , i ’ faith , in exactly the same degree ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: ne quíd magis simus ⋯ omnibus obníxe opibus
sections.translation: that there may be no further mischief , it befits us studiously to remember our duty by all means in our power .

sections.label: Section 1.1.47
sections.original: Placet ; táceo .
sections.translation: ’ Tis good ; I ’ m silenced .

sections.label: Section 1.1.47b
sections.original: At memineris fácito .
sections.translation: But do take care and remember it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48
sections.original: Nolo égo , soror , me credi esse immemorem viri ,
sections.translation: I do not wish , sister , to be thought to be unmindful of my husband ; nor has he thrown away the distinction that he conferred upon me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: nam pol mihi grata acceptaque eiust benignitas .
sections.translation: For , by my troth , his kindness is pleasing and delightful to me ; and , really , this choice of mine is not now irksome to me , nor is there any reason why I should wish to abandon this match . But , in fine , ’ tis placed in our father ’ s power [*] ; that must be done by us which our relatives enjoin .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: Scio , atque in cogitando maerore augeor ,
sections.translation: I know it , and in thinking of it I am overwhelmed with grief ; for already has he almost disclosed his sentiments .

sections.label: Section 1.1.57
sections.original: Igitur quaeramus , nobis quid facto usus sit .
sections.translation: Let us consider , then , what is necessary for us to do .

sections.label: Section 1.2.58
sections.original: Quí manet ut moneatur semper servos homo officium suom
sections.translation: The man in condition of a servant who always waits to be told his duty , and doesn ’ t remember to do it of his own accord , that servant , I say , is not of a deserving character .

sections.label: Section 1.2.60
sections.original: vos meministis quot kalendis petere demensum cibum :
sections.translation: You remember well on each returning Calends to ask for your allotment of provisions [*] ; why , then , do you less remember to do what is necessary to do about the house ? Now , therefore , if , when I return , the furniture shall not be set for me , each piece in its proper place , I ’ ll be putting you in mind with a bull ’ s hide remembrancer [*] . Not human beings seem to be living with me , but pigs .

sections.label: Section 1.2.65
sections.original: facite sultis , nitidae ut aedes meae sint , quom redeam domum .
sections.translation: Take care , if you please , that my house is clean , when I return home . I shall soon be back home ; I ’ m going to her house , to see my eldest daughter . If any one should enquire for me , call me thence , some of you ; or — I shall be here soon myself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.68
sections.original: Quid agimus , soror , si óffirmabit pater adversum nos ?
sections.translation: What are we to do , sister , if our father shall resolve against us ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.68b
sections.original: Pati
sections.translation: It befits us to submit to what he does whose power is the stronger .

sections.label: Section 1.2.70
sections.original: exorando , haud adversando sumendam operam censeo :
sections.translation: By entreating , not by opposing , I think we must use our endeavours . If with mildness we ask for favour , I trust to obtain it of him . Oppose him we cannot , without disgrace and extreme criminality ; I will neither do that myself , nor will I give you the advice to do it , but rather that we should entreat him . I know our family [*] ; he will yield to entreaty .

sections.label: Section 1.2.75
sections.original: Principium ego quo pacto cum illis occipiam , id ratiocinor :
sections.translation: In the first place , in what manner I should make a beginning with them , about that I am in doubt ; whether I should accost them in language couched in ambiguous terms , after this fashion , as though I had never pretended [*] anything at all against them , or whether as though I had heard that they were deserving of some censure against them ; whether I should rather try them gently or with threats . I know that there will be opposition ; I know my daughters right well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.80
sections.original: si manere hic sese malint potius quam alio nubere .
sections.translation: If they should prefer to remain here rather than to marry afresh , why , let them do so . What need is there for me , the term of my life run out , to be waging war with my children , when I think that they don ’ t at all deserve that I should do so ? By no means ; I ’ ll have no disturbances . But I think that this is the best thing to be done by me ; I ’ ll do thus ; I ’ ll pretend as though they had themselves been guilty of some fault ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: perplexabilitér earum hodie perpavefaciam pectora ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll terribly terrify their minds this day by some ambiguous expressions ; ana then , after that , as I shall feel disposed , I ’ ll disclose myself . I know that many words will be spoken ; I ’ ll go in . ( Goes to the door of PHILUMENA ’ S house .) But the door ’ s open .

sections.label: Section 1.2.88
sections.original: Certo enim míhi paternae vocis sonitus auris accidit .
sections.translation: Why , surely the sound of my father ’ s voice reached my ears .

sections.label: Section 1.2.89
sections.original: Is est ecastor . ferre advorsum homini occupemus osculum .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , ’ tis he ; let ’ s hasten to meet him with a kiss .

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: Salve , mi pater .
sections.translation: My father , my respects .

sections.label: Section 1.2.90b
sections.original: Et vos ambae . ílico agite assidite .
sections.translation: And to you the same . Away this instant , and be off from me ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.91
sections.original: Osculum —
sections.translation: One kiss .

sections.label: Section 1.2.91b
sections.original: Sat est ósculi mihi vostri .
sections.translation: I ’ ve had enough of your kissing .

sections.label: Section 1.2.91c
sections.original: Quí , amabo , pater ?
sections.translation: Prithee , father , why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.92
sections.original: Quia ita meae animae salsura evenit .
sections.translation: Because , as it is , the seasoning of your affection has reached my soul [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.92b
sections.original: Asside hic , pater .
sections.translation: Sit down here , father .

sections.label: Section 1.2.93
sections.original: Non sedeo istic , vos sedete ; ego sedero in subsellio .
sections.translation: I ’ ll not sit there ; do you sit down ; I ’ ll sit on the bench [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.94
sections.original: Mane pulvinum .
sections.translation: Wait till I fetch a cushion .

sections.label: Section 1.2.94b
sections.original: Bene procuras . mihi satis sic fultumst . sede .
sections.translation: You take kind care of me ; I ’ m nicely seated now as I am [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: Sine , pater .
sections.translation: Do let me , father .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95b
sections.original: Quid opust ?
sections.translation: What need is there ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.95c
sections.original: Opust .
sections.translation: There is need .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95d
sections.original: Mórem tibi geram . atque hoc est satis .
sections.translation: I ’ ll submit to you . ( Arranging the cushion .) Yes , this does very well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.96
sections.original: Numquam enim nímis curare possunt suom parentem filiae .
sections.translation: Why , daughters can never take too much care of their parent . Whom is it proper that we should esteem more dear than yourself ? And then , in the next place , father , our husbands , for whom you have chosen that we should be the mothers of families .

sections.label: Section 1.2.99
sections.original: Bonas ut aequomst facere facitis , quom tamen apsentis viros
sections.translation: You do as it is proper for good wives to do , in esteeming your husbands ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: proinde habetis quasi praesentes sint .
sections.translation: though absent , just as though they were present .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100b
sections.original: Pudicitiast , pater ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis propriety , father , for us to highly honor those who have chosen us as companions for themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.102
sections.original: Num quis hic ést alienus nostris dictis auceps auribus ?
sections.translation: Is there any other person here to listen with his ears [*] to our conversation ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.103
sections.original: Nullus praeter nosque teque .
sections.translation: There ’ s no one except us and yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103b
sections.original: Vostrum animum adhiberi volo ;
sections.translation: I wish your attention to be given ; for , unacquainted with female matters and ways ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: discipulus venio ad magistras : quibus matronas moribus ,
sections.translation: I come now as a pupil to you , my instructresses ; in order that each of you may tell me what endowments matrons ought to have , who are the best esteemed .

sections.label: Section 1.2.107
sections.original: Quid istuc est quod huc éxquaesitum mulierum mores venis ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the reason that you come hither to enquire about the ways of females ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.108
sections.original: Pol ego uxorem quaero , postquam vostra mater mortuast .
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m looking for a wife , as your mother ’ s dead and gone .
